data.title: XLV Vs. BME: Total Return Or Income, Which Healthcare Strategy Wins?
data.description:
A popular sector pick for 2026

I recently posted my analysis of which Health Care sector picks of the large cap S&P 500 (SP500) variety appeared cheap using a quantitative evaluation system. The Health Care sector has been one of the worst performers in the past 5 years and was previously thought of as a resilient defensive sector. I mean, society will never cease to require health care; hospital appointments are hard to book, business must be booming, right? If it were only that easy.

Health Care is one of the most regulated industries, both in terms of what drugs get approved and how long patents last before a generic version of a popular drug comes to market. The Trump 2.0 iteration has been tough on the Health Care sector names, viewing them as a part of the national inflation problem.

The sector, however, is now becoming part of a larger narrative for a mean reversion candidate in 2026. Let's see the 11 sectors of the economy and how they have performed over the last 5 years using the State Street sector select ETFs as proxies:

Ranked in order of 5 year total return performance:

Rank Sector ETF 5-Year Total Return
1 Energy (XLE) 161.6%
2 Technology (XLK) 135.9%
3 Industrials (XLI) 95.79%
4 Financials (XLF) 88.97%
5 Communication Services (XLC) 87.95%
6 Utilities (XLU) 62.60%
7 Consumer Discretionary (XLY) 53.02%
8 Health Care (XLV) 45.40%
9 Consumer Staples (XLP) 39.85%
10 Materials (XLB) 38.71%
11 Real Estate (XLRE) 37.67%

Out of the 11 sectors over the last 5 years, Health Care ranks 8th; much of this catch-up came in the last half of 2025 when the sector started to recover. I, personally, view it as one of the better sectors in terms of valuation. My previous article on the top 5 large-cap Health Care stocks revealed an average P/E ratio of 14.62 X FWD amongst a group consisting of Pfizer (PFE), Amgen (AMGN), Merck (MRK), Gilead (GILD), and Bristol Myers (BMY) weighted according to a factor scoring system.

This 5-stock portfolio was weighted exactly in this fashion :

During that short period, November 2025 to current, here were the results:

Needless to say, these weren't needle-in-a-haystack names; they are featured prominently in most sector funds related to Health Care. The sector is gaining momentum.

Today I'd like to explore some simple ways to invest in the sector. One is using the Health Care Select SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLV), and the other is an active closed-end fund by BlackRock, The BlackRock Health Sciences Trust (NYSE: BME)

The SPDR Healthcare Sector ETF

The SPDR sector ETFs are a no-frills way to invest in any sector of the US economy that an investor believes may outperform. State Street has an ETF for each, and the focus is to break apart the S&P 500 by its constituent sectors and then market-cap-weight those accordingly. It is one of the easiest ways to gain sector-specific performance with the thesis that momentum drives large capitalization companies higher if that sector maintains its popularity during a given year.

5 year dividend profile

Being that this is a collection of the sector with larger capitalization rates, that normally entails a smaller dividend [unless a sector is really beaten down]. The names that rise in capitalization see their yields fall by proxy. The sector ETF has maintained roughly a 1.4-1.8% average dividend yield over the past 5 years.

The holdings

The top ten holdings in this fund carry 57.66% of the portfolio weight. This is an index fund with a low expense ratio of .08%. The fund has a forward P/E ratio of 19.94 X, lower than the broader market but higher than the holdings in my previous large-cap Health Care sector analysis.

The BlackRock Health Sciences Closed End Fund

Two things we need to keep in mind off the bat: this is a closed-end fund, one that issues its shares once and then can pull in leverage if it needs to [borrowing like a corporation], and 2, it distributes large portions of the annual capital gains to investors. It relies on its NAV [net asset value], net investment income, and return of capital [ROC] as the source of capital to distribute to investors.

These funds can erode that NAV by paying too much out in years with negative capital appreciation and sometimes pay bonuses over trailing years when capital gains are larger than expected. Most payouts are done with US-based tax efficiency in mind. This fund, like other closed-end funds, can overwrite portions of its portfolio to generate income as well. This is the complete opposite of XLV from a management standpoint, as income distribution, combined with a bevy of active management tools, is at the disposal of the managers.

The fund has a bit over half a billion in assets under management [AUM] and is currently employing minimal amounts of leverage at .01%.

The trailing distribution is 7.51% and has a total gross expense ratio of 1.11%. This is high compared to traditional index funds and is the cost of active management. The fund manager in this instance acts as an engineer to both pick stocks and make sure that the capital being sent back to you is done so in a tax-efficient manner, with mainly long-term capital gains.

For those in retirement or semi-retirement using taxable brokerages to generate portfolio income, you know that the tax treatment of income is as important as the yield itself. The manager's ability to get you long-term capital gains or return of capital without eroding NAV is an essential skill in these vehicles. In the case of BME, 2025 was an efficient year, as we'll see in a moment.

5 year distribution profile

Distribution yields are up to near record levels over the last 5 years, as the Health Care sector and funds related to it have not been popular. This has allowed closed-end funds in this sector to sell at discounts to NAV, which increases your yield on cost.

The holdings

In this fund, the current holdings matter less than with XLV, as this is an active fund. Strategies may change from year to year, but this is a near 100% equity closed-end fund rather than a hybrid one with debt instruments and preferred equity within the holdings. I personally prefer closed-end funds that are all equity.

We can also see that the fund currently sells at a -5.97% discount to net asset value. The 52-week average discount is -7.41%; thus, this fund is starting to gain some positive investor momentum.

Tax profile

Below is the characterization of the monthly distributions paid by BME for 2025. Again, this is a monthly payer, which is also unique versus most sector ETFs.

All data from cefconnect

Category 2025 Total ($) Tax Treatment
Ordinary Income $0.1572 ordinary income
Long-Term Capital Gains $2.6729 Preferential LTCG rates
Short-Term Capital Gains $0.0530 Taxed as ordinary income
Return of Capital [ROC] $0.0000 Non-taxable (basis reduction)
Total Distributions $2.8831 Mixed character

92.7% is characterized as long-term capital gains for BME in 2025. Again, for those who either fully or partially utilize taxable brokerages to fund retirement, long-term capital gains get preferential treatment. In 2026, a couple married filing jointly [MFJ] can get up to $98,900 at the 0% tax bracket for long-term capital gains. This special bracket is reduced by any other earned or ordinary income but is still a primary reason why income investors can preserve taxable account portfolios in retirement by not paying much, if any, tax on long-term capital gains and qualified dividends.

Total return profiles

The 5 year total return

Over the past 5 years that saw Health Care have meager performance, XLV was able to hold up better than owning an active strategy for this sector.

The 10 year total return

Over the entire existence of the two funds, we see the active closed end fund actually outperforming the index fund by quite a bit.

BME's nav

BME's NAV is still below its 10 year high in 2021 but has been steadily recovering heading into the tail end of 2025. Better NAV [appreciation of the underlying stocks] should result in a better ability to raise distributions without harming the capital value of BME's portfolio.

Long term holistic observations

Backtesting these two funds from 2006 to year to date, we can see the below statistics from Portfolio Visualizer:

BME has a slightly lower max drawdown during this long backtest period but slightly higher standard deviations. These deviations are partially influenced by distributions, which can drop the share price as capital leaves the portfolio in larger amounts than it would in XLV. BME has a higher CAGR at 11.08% vs 10.17% for XLV in this backtest.

Risks and summary

The Health Care sector is moving toward the middle of the pack in terms of sector performers on a trailing 5-year basis. Risks still remain in patent cliffs and government regulations, which can change on a whim in the Trump administration.

BME tends to have a higher P/E ratio with higher momentum names toward the top and could be a better choice to catch a tailwind in Health Care should it arrive in 2026. In 2025, most of the distributions were long-term capital gains. I personally prefer active management in this sector, as companies have more variations than other sector brethren would. I also like a value oriented investment into the Health Care sector, with many household names selling at discounts to long term valuations, but XLV is not the fund to capture that either.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JPMorgan Q4 Earnings: Compelling Valuation At Current Levels, But Risks Linger
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My last article on JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) was an earnings preview on the company’s 3rd quarter. I had a BUY rating on the stock.

Since my article was published in October 2025, the bank’s shares have gained 1.92%, underperforming the S&P 500, which gained 3.73% during the same period.

JPM kickstarted the Q4 earnings season yesterday. Overall, while it was a strong quarter, there were some weaknesses in certain areas. The bank also offered additional commentary on its future, especially after the bank added a significant asset to its portfolio.

In this article, therefore, I analyze the bank’s Q4 numbers and evaluate the key takeaways from the quarter.

Fourth Quarter Highlights

JPM had a decent finish to the year, with a beat on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $46.77 billion, up 6.9% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $520 million. Q4 Non-GAAP EPS came in at $5.23, up 8.73% y/y and comfortably beating analyst estimates by $0.37. The bank’s book value per share came in at $126.99, up 9% y/y and the tangible book value per share came in at $107.6, which represents a y/y growth of 10.6%. Net interest income, a key metric for the bank, came in at $25.1 billion, up 7% y/y. Provisions for credit losses came in at $4.66 billion, marginally lower than street estimates of $4.68 billion. Finally, both the Return on Equity [ROE] at 15% and the Return on Tangible Equity [ROTCE] at 14.5% missed analyst estimates by 0.7% and 0.3% respectively.

As for guidance, the company now expects 2026 net interest income (excl. markets) to come in at approximately $95 billion. The bank expects overall Net Interest Income to come in at $103 billion, which is ahead of street estimates of $100 billion.

Investment Banking Revenues Show Sharp Decline, But It’s Likely to Be a One-Off

One of the distinct takeaways from JPM’s Q4 numbers was the sharp decline in the revenue growth of the Commercial & Investment Banking [CIB] segment. While the y/y growth was still positive and in double digits, there was a substantial decline compared to last quarter. Moreover, the growth was the second lowest in the last five quarters, which makes it stand out like a sore thumb. Sequentially, the segment saw a negative growth, as seen below, its worst performance in the last five quarters.

In my view, while this is concerning, it is likely to be a one-off, especially since the primary driver behind the growth decline was the timing of the deals being pushed to 2026. Furthermore, there was also the matter of lower revenues generated from credit trading. At this point in time, I wouldn’t worry too much about the growth decline, especially since the environment looks ripe for dealmaking. Furthermore, 2026, in my view, is likely to be yet another volatile year due to a myriad of uncertainties, which should be a tailwind for JPM’s trading revenues.

Overall, while investors should certainly monitor the CIB’s performance more closely, especially after a downbeat Q4, there are enough tailwinds to indicate that the segment’s growth could get back to its upward trajectory.

Apple Card Acquisition To Take Two Years to Complete, But Monitoring Its Impact Must Start Today

One of the biggest developments related to JPM recently was its announcement that it was acquiring Apple’s credit card program from (GS). While it’s a substantial win for JPM as the acquisition is expected to further boost its dominant position in consumer credit, in my view, this is a risky bet and one that investors need to monitor.

For instance, while GS is selling the program at a discount in excess of $1 billion to JPM to account for the higher-than-average exposure to subprime borrowers and a higher-than-industry-average delinquency rate, these elevated factors are certainly a risk that JPM investors would need to factor in and closely monitor in the coming quarters. JPM management announced that they had already accounted for these factors when they increased their provision for credit losses. However, given that the integration is expected to take 2 years to complete, investors need to monitor for any further changes that management would make to the credit loss provisions in the coming quarters if and when more information comes to light.

JPM has a lot to win through the acquisition of Apple's (AAPL) credit card program, but there are risks that investors need to monitor closely in the coming months. These risks are likely to get more prevalent, especially when one factors in the latest onslaught from the Trump Administration on the credit card industry.

Valuation

Forward P/B Approach

Price Target

$358.00

Projected Forward P/B Multiple

2.4x

Projected FY26 Book Value Per Share

$148.98

Source: LSEG Data and Author’s Calculations

According to LSEG Data, the company currently trades at a forward P/B of 2.4x, marginally lower compared to GS and (MS), but significantly more expensive than the likes of (WFC) and (C). This is consistent with JPM’s historical trend of trading at a higher multiple compared to other money center banks, given its more profitable and diversified structure. As such, I have assumed this multiple for my calculations.

JPM’s book value of equity at the end of FY25 stood at $342.4 billion (126.99 book value per share x 2,696.2 common shares outstanding). As mentioned earlier, the company expects Net Interest Income [NII] to come in at $103 billion. After accounting for a tax rate of 24.1%, the NII after taxes comes in at $78.18 billion. The bank’s FY25 dividend growth rate stood at 20.83% y/y. Assuming the company maintains this growth rate for FY26, total dividends paid out for FY26 are projected to come in at $18.9 billion. The projected book value per share for FY26 is then projected to come in at $148.98 [(342.4 + 78.18 – 18.9) / 2.6962], representing a y/y growth of 17.3%.

A forward P/B of 2.4x and a projected FY26 book value per share of $148.98 results in a price target of $358, which translates to an upside of a little more than 15% from current levels.

While there is a healthy upside from current levels, the stock’s short-term momentum remains weak, as evidenced by the C rating on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum scale. When one combines this with the stalling growth at the [CIB] segment that I mentioned earlier, I do anticipate weakness in the short term. Therefore, while I am maintaining my BUY rating on the stock, a dollar-cost-averaging strategy is recommended when it comes to JPM.

Risk Factors

One of the risk factors to my bull case for investors to consider would be a scenario whereby the bank has to further increase its provision for credit losses, especially as a result of the Apple Card acquisition. The bank has already added $2.2 billion in loan-loss reserves to cover the risk and also took a one-time EPS hit of $0.60 per share. Any further increases would be concerning.

Then there’s the Trump administration's recent proposal to cap credit card loan rates at 10%, which would be a significant hit to the bank. During the earnings call, JPM CFO Jeremy Barnum warned that the 10% cap would adversely affect the operations of the bank and that it would have to cut back the amount of credit it offers. This would also be a significant risk factor to my bull case.

Finally, competition among banks is getting fierce, which means that expense growth is climbing. JPM is no different, as reflected in the expense growth outlook for 2026. Whether the expense growth can lead to meaningfully higher revenue growth compared to 2025 would be yet another factor that investors need to monitor.

Concluding Thoughts

JPM had a solid quarter, with a beat on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. While the bank had an overall solid year, Q4 did show certain areas of weakness. The decline in revenue growth of the [CIB] segment is concerning, although in my view, the Q4 decline is likely to be a one-off given the favorable environment for dealmaking.

JPM’s acquisition of AAPL’s credit card program is certainly a huge boost for the bank. However, there are risks that investors need to closely monitor in the coming months as the bank integrates the program into its operations. The Trump Administration’s 10% cap on credit cards is also a significant headwind should it come to fruition.

From a valuation perspective, the recent loss in momentum in the stock and the post-earnings selloff have made the stock attractive, as evidenced by the upside of more than 15% from current levels. Despite maintaining my BUY rating on the stock, I strongly recommend a dollar-cost-averaging strategy when it comes to JPM given the lingering risks and the continued weakness in momentum.

2026 should be yet another solid year for JPM in my opinion, driven by a favorable dealmaking environment and continued growth in Wealth Management. However, the risks surrounding the banking environment in general, and JPM in particular are too significant to overlook.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P 500: Don't Be Misguided By These Charts Again In 2026
data.description:
S&P 500: You must have seen these charts in 2025

In the recent few years (especially during 2025), I have been seeing these following charts more and more often as all major market indices (including DJI, SP500, NASDAQ, and RTY) kept creating new record high prices and also valuation multiples. I bet chances are that you’ve seen your fair share of them (or some other variations of them).

In the off chance that you are seeing them for the first time, let me quickly walk you through two of them. The first one shown below is taken from LPL Financial, and the second one is taken from Lazy Portfolio ETF. Both chart show – or more precisely, intend to show, as I see it - a negative correlation of the SP500’s future return with respect to its current P/E and/or its past return. In many of the articles I’ve read about these charts, the authors rely on them to support a bullish view against the current equity market, given how expensive its P/E is and how quickly its prices have appreciated in recent years. To wit, the SP500 ended 2025 with a P/E of around 30.8x and currently hovers around 31.4x, as shown in the first chart, some of the highest levels in the index’s record history. As shown in the second chart, the SP500 has also posted an annualized return of 14.72% in the past 10 years as of Dec 31, 2025, making the past 10 one of the most terrific bull runs since the years leading to the dot-com bubble.

The statistics displayed in these charts seem to be solid or even irrefutable. Yet, if you’ve taken them at their face value to bet against the equity market in recent years, your investment results would be horrible. In the rest of this article, I will explain why this is the case and why they would keep generating misleading signals in 2026 again.

Sample independence

The above charts and their interpretation (at least the ones I’ve read) are very problematic. When a message is wrapped in statistics, the message sometimes can become more dangerous, as many readers are untrained on - or have forgotten about - the basic mathematical concepts behind the statistics. Among the many issues with the above charts and their interpretation, I will focus on the top 2 in my view to keep the discussion within a reasonable length. Both are rooted in fundamental statistics and mathematical laws. The first one involves the requirements of sample independence in any regression analysis, and the second one involves the laws about reversion to the mean.

Let me start with sample independence. Whenever I see a large amount of data neatly falling on a fit, I can't help but feel impressed or even awed. And that is precisely the feeling I had when I took my first glance at the above charts. I bet many of you felt the same way. However, the top two requirements for any data fitting are A) a sufficiently large sample and B) independent samples. It’s debatable if the above charts may have met requirement A or not, but they currently don’t meet requirement B.

Both charts use rolling 10-year return as the correlation target, which inevitably relies on heavily overlapping data points for many reasons. First, for any given period, 9 out of 10 years of rolling returns overlap with the next period. Second, the return for many years can be defined only by a few consequential events. For example, the burst of the dot-com bubble defined the returns between the 2000s and the 2008 crisis, which in turn defined the returns for many years after that. Due to these reasons, many – if not most – of the data points used in these charts are independent.

As two possible ways to illustrate my above points, the first chart below processed past returns with only non-overlapping periods, and the second chart shows 10-year rolling return results from a much wider window of more than 150 years, from 1871 to 2025. In both cases, the results now show little correlation – if any at all.

Reversion to the mean

A good amount of credibility of the above charts is also derived from their alignment with the concept of RTTM (revision to the mean, or regression to the mean). In my experience, RTTM is among the most popular and also most abused concepts in investing. Its popularity stems from its intuitive sense (an extreme event is likely to be followed by a less extreme event).

However, the validity of RTTM is limited by a few fundamental factors. I will refrain from detailed elaborations and only cite the gist. Otherwise, this could become a mathematical lecture. For RTTM to apply, the samples have to A) be truly random, obviously, B) come from the same distribution, and C) have a static mean to revert to. The equity market satisfies none of these requirements in my view. The lasting and structural impact of a single event (like the dot-com bubble mentioned above) for years is good evidence against A and B. As for C, I don’t see why the P/E or return of the equity market should have a static mean at all while many of the underlying forces are evolving unidirectionally instead of randomly – at least within the timeframes used in the charts shown above. These forces include technological advancements, government fiscal and monetary policies, global trade networks, etc.

As an example to illustrate the impact of government fiscal and monetary policies, the chart below shows the long-term trend of 10-year treasury rates. As seen, since the 1970s, 10-year treasury rates have averaged 5.82%. The rates peaked at over 15% in the 1980s and have been on a secular decline since then. The current reading of around 4.2% is relatively low compared to the long term.

Furthermore, I feel comfortable sticking my neck out and making a strong prediction here – I don’t expect to see 10-year treasury rates as high as those observed in the 1970~1990 again in my lifetime. BTW, I am in my mid-forties and I actually manage my portfolio according to this prediction as detailed in my recent article cautioning readers against the 60-40 allocation model.

There are many reasons that gave me the comfort – and confidence – to make this prediction, such as the evolved role of the Fed, the country’s tax policies, etc. As shown in the chart below, corporate tax rates have declined secularly since WWII from rates above 50% to around 20% in recent years (see the next chart). According to data from the Peterson Foundation, the expansion of our tax base was not able to offset the decrease of tax rates. As a net result, the US now collects lower corporation taxes (that is, as a percentage of its GDP) among developed countries. In 2022, for example, the most recent year for which such data is available, U.S. corporate tax revenues accounted for just 1.3% of U.S. GDP, while the average is 3.2% among the 13 developed economies.

With the combination of much debt (either in absolute amount or in comparison to the past) and the current level of tax income, I see little odds for treasury rates to “revert” back to the elevated levels we’ve seen in the past. Risk-free rates serve as the most important and fundamental parameter governing equity valuation and capital deployment in my model. With the above substantial and structural change in this parameter, I see little reason why there should be a static mean for equity P/E and/or return potential to regress to.

Other risks and final thoughts

Besides the above change in risk-free rates, there are many other factors that have also undergone substantial and structural changes. Global trade and technological advancements are two other notable factors. As an example, according to data provided by Apollo Academy, during this 22-year period from 2002 to 2024, the share of S&P 500 revenues from outside the US annually has expanded from about 35% to about 41%. If we further expand our timeframe back to the 1950s, S&P 500 company revenue is almost entirely generated domestically. I certainly A) believe that such an expanded reach to the global market should make SP500 companies more valuable and have better growth potential, and B) consider such change to be unidirectional rather than random. Near-term geopolitical conflicts and trade disputes (like what we are going through now) could temporarily pause the expansion or even set it back a bit. But these are only near-term effects and don’t change the long-term trend in my view.

The continued improvement of technology and productivity is an even more powerful and consistent force. As an example, the bar chart below is provided by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. It illustrates productivity changes in the nonfarm business sector from 1947 to 2025. As seen, the productivity change has fluctuated from era to era. The highest average annual growth occurred between 2001 and 2007 at 2.8%, overlapping with the widespread use of the internet. Overall, the long-term rate for the entire nearly 80-year span remains steady at an average annual percent change of 2.1%. And more importantly, despite fluctuations, the productivity change has ALWAYS been positive when measured during a reasonably long period of time. Whether the ongoing AI revolution will lead to another period of unusually high productivity again like those experienced during 2001-2007 is yet to be seen (and I feel optimistic). But even if AI does not work out, I see no reason at all for the productivity gain to stop.

To wrap up, my goal here is quite simple. I wanted to caution investors against the use – of misuse - of past correlations to make future investment decisions. In particular, if you’ve followed the correlations shown in the charts that I’ve used to open this article and bet against the equity market, you would have suffered miserable results in recent years. I see no difference for 2026, as these charts and their popular interpretations are fundamental flaws. More particularly for 2026, I feel it is especially timely to point out their flaws given my outlook of risk-free rates and the potential of AI-driven productivity gain.

data.title: Newell Brands: Baby-Step Recovery, But Shareholders Could Get Rubbed Away - Strong Sell
data.description:
Intro

Newell Brands (NWL) is a consumer goods company that designs, manufactures, and markets a wide array of everyday household products across its segments Home & Commercial (kitchen appliances, fragrance, and cleaning), Learning & Development (writing tools and baby gear), and Outdoor & Recreation. NWL is operating globally with about two thirds of sales being generated in North America and a third coming from international markets.

The company owns brands like Sharpie and Paper Mate for writing, Graco for baby products, Yankee Candle for home fragrance, Rubbermaid for storage, Calphalon and Crock-Pot for kitchenware, and Coleman for outdoor gear.

A deciding factor for its current vast portfolio lineup — but also for its stock price decline — was the $16 billion Jarden acquisition in 2016. The mega deal in hindsight is equal to NWL stock’s all-time high that is has never seen again.

NWL fell sharply amid a deal-induced, massive shareholder value destruction and a huge debt load from which it has not recovered fully to this day.

While Newell Brands has been suffering from tariffs and the resulting cost as well as supply chain disruptions in 2025, this is more a welcome near-term excuse. I rate NWL stock a strong sell, as I cannot rule out that this company will be rubbed away entirely, given its dire financial situation.

Crawling towards a turnaround under pressure

I am analyzing the latest Q3 2025 results (conference call transcript here) as well as the appearance of NWL on the Morgan Stanley Global Consumer & Retail Conference from early December 2025, together with long-term financial data.

In Q3 2025, Newell Brands posted a 7.2% net sales decline to $1.8 billion for the quarter, with core sales down 7.4%. This deeply red top line missed expectations – a "dynamic and challenging" macro environment was given as the main reason. To the defense of the company, despite my overall negative view, the macro situation has indeed been challenging for many producers and sellers of especially discretionary products.

Gross margins dropped from 34.9% to 34.1%, while operating margins improved modestly from 6.2% to 6.6% — however, only on a reported basis, the normalized figure for the latter still showed a decline, from 9.5% to 8.9%. The details are not even that important, as the key message remains that NWL is under heavy pressure, sales-wise, but also regarding margins.

Importantly, this near-term decline is strongly impacted by last year’s tariff chaos. But looking at the long-term chart, here for gross margins, it looks more like just a drop in the bucket. Gross margins have been falling for years now, especially since the disastrous acquisition of Jarden.

A quick look at operating margins confirms this big-picture view:

Before I go into more detail regarding the ongoing turnaround and to highlight the long-term issues the company is plagued with, NWL has been carrying around a hefty debt load.

Below, we can see net debt in orange and cash in green. Net debt includes leasing, but it is a comparatively small amount, as the orange bar mainly consist of net financial debt that has exploded with the Jarden acquisition.

While net debt has been reduced substantially from $11 billion to ~$5 billion, we will see soon that this is still an enormous amount, potentially too much for NWL down the road.

To conclude, here are equity (purple line) as well as goodwill (orange) and intangible assets (green). My message here is, the Jarden deal was a financial disaster for Newell shareholders. Equity was inflated with intangible assets that are hard to value. Newell substantially overpaid which resulted in quick and painful asset write downs in the billions.

Maybe the only good news is that intangible assets and goodwill have been reduced meaningfully so what was said before belongs to the past. However, both together are still considerably higher than equity — roughly two times. And both together still offer up to around $5 billion of additional write-down risk. The balance sheet is weak and fragile, but I will recap the debt situation further below.

Positives during the quarter shone through in some targeted efforts (what I meant with baby steps in my headline) as follows. The company focussed on productivity and overhead savings to cut costs, protecting margins as best as possible. Surprisingly, market share gains were reported in the baby category despite aggressive pricing, stemming from a heavy reliance on sourcing from Asia at the time of tariff implementation.

Normalized figures (as seen on the press release screenshot above) like gross margins were higher than the reported ones by 40 bps — the normalization was done to show ex-tariff figures. While nice to know, we need to deal with the reality.

This was practically it regarding the positives. Operating cash flow fell sharply compared to last year from $346 million to just $103 million. Net debt at first glance reads like it has been reduced from $5.0 billion to $4.8 billion — but this is just on a gross level. Cash has fallen in tandem, so that net debt remained almost unchanged.

To show that debt remains a high burden, here is the long-term cash flow picture. Operating cash flow has been falling, while Capex was simultaneously reduced modestly. With the exception of FY 2022 and the inflation shock, FCF came in positively each year, despite a falling tendency. This chart goes until FY 2024.

Zooming in to the quarterly view (trailing twelve months each), we can see the clear downtrend in cash generation. Of note, Q1 2025 saw negative FCF — this was before tariffs were announced. Thereafter, FCF dropped stronger, but overall and looking over mor than just a few quarters, I do not see a sign of strong execution as cash flow has been falling over many quarters. A sudden caught-by-surprise moment due to tariffs, reads more like a welcome excuse, but it is not the root cause for the situation the company is in. In my view, NWL has been showing inherent weakness for longer than just the last few tumultuous quarters.

Given, headwinds loomed large with temporarily escalating tariffs creating a negative $180 million cash impact, pressuring margins and sparking retailer inventory cuts. On top, international markets like Brazil (down 25%) and Argentina stalled due to political and tariff turmoil. These factors, plus broader category weakness (down 2-3%), overshadowed innovation and cost-cutting wins, painting a picture of a company grinding through external storms but struggling to fully offset them.

The guidance given with the Q3 2025 results gave few reasons to celebrate. Both, net and core sales were guided to be down for Q4 and the full year. Operating cash flow was seen at $250-300 million for the full year which, if hit, would result in FCF of around zero, maybe slightly positive, depending on the exact outcome (based on Capex for the last twelve months).

Just in brief, fast-forward to the December 2025 Morgan Stanley conference, and before we conclude and piece the key items together, Newell announced a new productivity plan slashing 10% of professional roles and closing 20 Yankee Candle stores. These aggressive cost-cutting efforts target $110-130 million in annual savings to complement prior complexity reductions (e.g., SKUs down 80%). This confirmed Q3's overhead reduction progress. As a result, the CFO eyes a 50-basis-point operating margin gain in 2026 toward a 12-15% long-term target (not next year and after tariff normalization).

This sounds like management being aware of the headwinds and acting in the right manner to finally manage the almost decade-long turnaround. Yet skepticism is not unwarranted. Newell's leverage, my main point of caution, remains stubbornly high at 5x. However, this is just to EBITDA, in my view a figure of little use, despite it being widely used. Free cash flow is expected to be, at best, a bit above zero this year. Even if next year cost savings kick in, we are still looking at a figure in the low hundred millions — breadcrumbs vs. $4.6 billion net debt. And this assumes that consumer sentiment does not worsen.

The good news is, in 2026 no financial debt is due, creating some breathing room and time to accomplish the envisioned progress. However, starting with 2027 and especially in 2028, considerable amount of debt will be due for refinancing. A full repayment realistically will not be possible, while refinancing puts the company at the mercy of creditors. It could be a successful gamble, refinancing at lower interest rates eventually, yes. But this is uncertain. Depending on how the company will be doing operationally, it is possible that cost of debt rises, when no one will be willing to give the company money for low interest rates, given the risk profile.

And, interest expense are indeed pressing, eating the majority of operating earnings. This is not a low-risk setup, to the contrary, a very tight-knit one where errors or timing shifts can become painful.

This self-inflicted scar from the Jarden deal, the huge debt load, is unrelated to tariffs, as balance sheet quality has been sacrificed already long before. This is draining financial flexibility and increasing dependency on near-perfect execution in a soft consumer environment where low-income households slashed spending, especially on discretionary items.

But it could get even worse: If tariffs get reversed via a highly anticipated pending Supreme Court decision, something seen as a positive for the economy and consumer companies, exactly this could backflip for Newell Brands which rearranged some of its supply chains. Imported rivals could suddenly regain cost edges, undercutting Newell's domestic premium.

So all in all, we have a challenging consumer environment, a highly-levered company with weak cash generation, and very high pressure to execute. The margin of safety is thin, as just small missteps, whether self-inflicted or externally caused, can bring the company into serious issues. Financial flexibility is limited and starting from 2027, debt refinancing risks mount. If NWL does not manage to post positive free cash flow on a consistent basis, it could be a very tight race, especially if consumer sentiment worsens and / or the competition manages to strongly undercut NWL, destroying the latter’s margin-recovery ambitions.

Accordingly, NWL is a strong sell for me, as I cannot rule out even a bankruptcy, given the extreme leverage.

Risks to my Thesis

Risks to this bearish thesis include a sharper-than-expected category rebound from low-income stimulus and wage gains, potentially amplifying Newell's cost-cutting tailwinds. Stronger international stabilization, especially in Latin America, could restore consistent core growth and ease tariff dependency. Finally, accelerated productivity yields might exceed expectations, fueling faster margin expansion and cash flow reaching strongly positive territory to tackle leverage.

Conclusion

In sum, Newell's operational grind persists amid self-made burdens, only intensified (but not caused) by tariffs, warranting a strong sell rating.

data.title: Pearson: Solid Business Priced Too Optimistically
data.description:
U.K.-based education provider Pearson (PSO) has seen its share price move up in recent years and some key business metrics also point to an improving performance.

I last covered the name three years ago in my sell note Pearson: Expensive For What It Is, since when it has moved up 22% according to SA data, though I note that the London-listed sterling-denominated share has gone up only 15% since that piece was published.

At that point I considered Pearson to be a solid business with some highly attractive business units, but overpriced in part due to the uncertain business benefit of the company’s increased focus on education.

I continue to hold that view, and it is even more pronounced now given that share price increase.

Performance Has Basically Been Fine

Last October the company released a trading update for the first nine months of 2025, the most recent indication of how it is doing. That revealed low single-digit percentage sales growth for the period.

On balance I would say that is pretty hard to get excited about. The English language learning performance was actually an improvement from the first half, so hopefully that business is on track.

The company is on track to deliver its full-year guidance. It raised its interim dividend by 5%.

2025 saw 4% Underlying Sales Revenue Growth

The company delivered a 2025 trading update this week. Full-year results are due on 27 February.

Underlying sales revenue growth was 4%, accelerating to 8% in the final quarter, which bodes well as we begin 2026. Across the full year, the strongest growth rate came from the virtual learning division.

The company had cash conversion of over 95%, suggesting that operating cash flow will again come in strongly.

Profits Have Been Rising, But Long-Term Performance is Uneven

The past few years have seen profits rise sequentially, which is positive. Free cash flows have also been on the up.

To some extent this reflects the strategic restructuring and refocus of the company over the past decade.

However, Pearson has also only slowly gotten its mojo back financially post-pandemic. Revenues remain smaller – a strategic choice not necessarily to be regretted in a company slimmed down from what it once was – while last year’s profit was below 2018’s and free cash flow only a little ahead of it. 2019 was worse than 2018, indicative of Pearson’s inconsistent financial performance in the years prior to the pandemic.

I think that inconsistency remains. Seen positively, the run over the past few years of increasing profits and free cash flows could be interpreted as a sign that the strategic shift has paid off and the operational focus has improved. Personally, I think it is too early to declare that. The results basically took a knock during the pandemic and have been on a long, slow recovery.

I continue to see a risk of cyclicality in at least some of the Pearson businesses. That helps explain some of the historically mixed performance and could lead to similar in future, in my view.

Valuation Continues to Look Stretched

Pearson has a market cap of £6.9bn. At the half year point it reported net debt of £1.0.

That makes for an enterprise value of £7.9bn. Operating cash flow in the first half was £126m. Extrapolating in a straight line to a full year figure, that would make for £252m meaning the current enterprise value is around 31 times operating cash flow.

That continues to look unjustifiably high for me. Sure, Pearson has some attractive IP that ought to give it pricing power. It had a 9.6% net profit margin in the first half. That is fine, but it is not especially juicy. Meanwhile, the revenue growth rates are tepid.

So can the company justify an enterprise value of 31 times operating cash flow?

To my mind, no. One counterargument is that AI may take out a lot of costs, e.g. for education provision, boosting margins. That could go either way, though: It could lead to more price competition from rivals, or a devaluation of the product in general (look at the 99% fall in the share price of Chegg (CHGG) over the past five years). For now, AI remains an unproven long-term impact when it comes to Pearson’s financial performance, and I do not think the share price merits a premium on the basis of potential AI upside, for now.

Accordingly, I continue to rate Pearson as a “sell” on the basis of its high valuation.

data.title: Something Is Happening With Bitcoin That I Would Have Never Expected: The BTCO Case
data.description:
Today something is happening on Bitcoin that I haven’t seen for quite some time. Something that naturally is also reflected in the ETFs that replicate its performance, in this sense also on the Invesco Galaxy Bitcoin ETF (BTCO).

I am referring to 2 very interesting divergences:

  • the one with the S&P 500
  • the one with global liquidity.

These are 2 elements that can easily be interpreted negatively: as a relative weakness of Bitcoin. And yet, personally, I interpret them positively. And before explaining why …

What is BTCO

BTCO is a spot Bitcoin ETF, with the objective of replicating the spot price of Bitcoin, net of costs.

These amount to an expense ratio of 0.25%, fairly competitive, if one considers a bid/ask prem/disc of -0.02% and a bid/ask spread of 0.08%, a sign of positive liquidity.

In this sense, naturally, the number of bitcoins per share decreases over time, even if Bitcoin rises. This is because each Sponsor Fee is paid by selling bitcoin, generating an effect of structural economic dilution and inevitable underperformance vs BTC spot in the long term.

For replication it uses the Lukka Prime Reference Rate (USD) mechanism; essentially it has prices aggregated from multiple exchanges to determine a fair value and then the NAV that moves synchronously with the underlying. It then uses an external custody mechanism at Coinbase Custody Trust Company, LLC.

Peer comparison

For completeness, I’m also sharing my peer table, although I want to point out that, after several comparative analyses, I’ve come to the conclusion that the key decision lever should be fees.

Specifically, BTCO does not have fees that are clearly higher than peers with larger AUM, such as FBTC in this respect, and IBIT. For this reason, in terms of performance there are no major deviations. To go deeper, I invite you to read here.

Something is happening that I didn’t expect

Those who follow me know that I usually analyze Bitcoin’s performance in comparative terms. I don’t appreciate the maximalist dynamic that sees Bitcoin as “the asset that will save us all no matter what.” Simply, it is an asset that, by following this narrative, is achieving great results, and as an analyst, I simply go along with it. To enter the market through ETFs, in the past I have oriented myself by following two parallels that have always brought me great satisfaction.

1. Bitcoin & S&P 500

Bitcoin, therefore the ETFs that use it as the underlying (in this case BTCO), for me is a high beta solution of the S&P 500 (and of the Nasdaq index). It means that it amplifies rises and falls in the same way as a technology stock (basically). Or rather … this is what has guided my research over the past years. But look at what happened today.

There has been a strong divergence between Bitcoin and the performance of traditional markets. And this, in my experience, and in my opinion, can be read in 2 ways:

  • A positive gap between prices that will facilitate Bitcoin’s growth.
  • A negative gap that anticipates a collapse of the S&P 500.

But let’s move on to the second …

2. Bitcoin and global liquidity

Bitcoin has a strength (not so common): it has a limited, or better said, predefined supply. Being therefore a “fixed-supply” asset, and freely accessible, it means that if liquidity in circulation increases, the price undergoes (in my opinion) a natural revaluation. For this reason there is a tight correlation between Bitcoin and money in circulation M2 (as is also the case for the S&P 500).

And it so happens that this is at all-time highs, while the price of Bitcoin instead is declining. Well … in my opinion, this is an important asymmetry to consider.

My opinion on BTCO

Does it make sense to expect a decline in the S&P 500? Certainly, the “unpredictable” exogenous shock is always around the corner, but if as an analyst I had to simply rely on today’s expectations, I don’t see the reason. Revenues and earnings are expected to grow strongly according to FactSet.

In the same way, liquidity is expected to expand on multiple fronts:

  • Fed cutting rates, especially after the DOJ accusations against Powell.
  • The unconditional QE of the US government with Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.
  • And the resumption of monthly expansions of the Fed’s balance sheet.

And if these 2 elements are considered sufficient drivers to describe Bitcoin’s performance (as has been the case in the recent period), I think it is more likely that the gap anticipates potential recoveries by Bitcoin, rather than a contraction of the general market.

Risk

Naturally, this is a forecast based on two arbitrary, and not absolute, conventions. In the sense that it is assumed that Bitcoin maintains an anchoring to these correlations, simply because in the past it has been so: this also means assuming that market habits never change. But this in itself is inconsistent with the very nature of the market. Just think of when Bitcoin was instead called “digital gold” and reacted positively to systemic shocks and geopolitical risks; which is completely inconsistent with the “new conception” of Bitcoin (the one I presented here). In fact, exactly the opposite would happen. So? The risk is that Bitcoin simply changes its conception.

How do I manage this risk? well, I work on the technical side. BTCO today is in a price range on which I can try to set accumulation strategies with controlled risk. Quite simply, in the area below 50 RSI points within the range and slightly below it, I can try to accumulate a position, to be managed with controlled risk.

Conclusion

These 2 divergences encourage me to maintain a positive rating (BUY) on BTCO. Why?

  • Looking at the nature of the ETF, I consider it competitive in the market.

  • Looking at intermarket analysis, it seems it can be positively influenced by the S&P 500 and the liquidity boom.

  • Looking at the chart, it is located in an area where I can try to work.

data.title: Gold Isn't Near A Peak
data.description:
I've been going on about gold for over a year now, pounding the table for all the reasons why it makes sense for precious metals to be on a generational bull run (and now industrial metals as well, hello copper).

And boy -- it has been on a significant bull run. In the last five years, the low was $1,627, and the price today sits close to $4,600.

That chart makes the ascent look violent, and it was by gold market standards, but looking at a very long timeframe log chart gives some more perspective about the cycles and where we are in the current cycle. The gold bubble in the late 70s-early 80s that folks keep warning about was much more violent, and for good reason -- we had just come off the gold standard, inflation was hitting double digits, and then the Arab oil embargo hit to cause an absolute take-off in commodities in '73.

That divergence from the 2-year average may be discouraging for some, but I see it as confirmation of the existing bull market. That kind of divergence is to be expected when metals rally like they have, and this chart doesn't look so overextended to me.

The Drivers of Gold Are Not Gone

The primary drivers of gold, in contrast with a commodity like oil, are namely monetary and hedging-related. When capital flees the dollar due to increased political and economic uncertainty in the US, gold jumps. The only consistent, major detractor from gold's returns from last year was momentum trading, or profit taking.

As the US Dollar dove 10% last year, traders piled into gold as a hedge against further currency debasement. Risk and uncertainty in world governments, as well as in the equities market -- also at all-time highs -- was also a significant driver.

When tracking investment flows, we can see that ETF investors have been slow to the punch. Speculation has not been driving this rally as much as some would have us believe; ETF holdings of gold are just now reaching the levels they struck in 2020.

In my last column on gold itself, "Gold Has A New Buyer, A Different Kind Of Central Bank," I covered how demand from central banks has been driving much of the price, as foreign banks de-dollarize and sell treasuries and dollars for gold and silver. This has been going on since 2022, when the US weaponized the dollar against Russia, punishing them monetarily for their belligerence in Ukraine.

We can see the major changes in how gold performs against other major safe haven assets from before to after 2022 -- the dollar is no longer where people flee when geopolitics gets spicy. It's gold, Swiss Franc, Japanese Yen, and US Treasuries.

This shift has resonated with traders, but also with central banks at large. They have been stockpiling gold like nobody's business in the last few years. When showing the major central bank buyers of gold, we get a list that is dominated by BRICS+ and aligned economies. Highlighted names below represent countries in BRICS+ (e.g., China, Russia, and Indonesia) or have governments aligned with BRICS geopolitically (e.g., Cambodia Serbia).

Emerging economies are moving faster than developed economies in gold accumulation, with the share of gold relative to foreign reserves in emerging markets having doubled in a time when developed economies have increased their allocations by 50%. Both are advancing quickly, but emerging economies are moving faster.

Gold vs. Oil

There's been a divergence between precious metals and the rest of the commodities bucket, and it's especially pronounced in oil, which had a terrible 2025.

Oil has had little love for a lot of reasons, namely the global oil glut that has gripped us after OPEC+ production hikes last year, and now with the promise of Venezuelan crude coming into the US (though I personally think we're years away from full production capacity), it's unlikely that oil prices will catch up to the broader commodities basket or to gold anytime soon without fresh catalysts that drive shortage. Supply seems to be growing faster than demand, especially considering how much pressure there is on data centers to be powered by natural gas and nuclear power, neither of which will provide much demand for oil.

This divergence of commodities has bled into the stock market, with energy firms trailing gold miners at the worst levels since 2020, when oil futures literally went negative. It's at the same levels we saw in 2011, the last time gold peaked.

But I'm not a believer that what goes up must come down (or vice versa), and this ratio could hold for a while. I actually expect it to correct with the outperformance of energy stocks, which I detailed more in my 2026 sector outlook article.

Gold vs. Silver

There has been much talk about the gold-to-silver ratio, and I'm guilty of this too. Back in April, I wrote about the historic moment where the ratio hit 100:

Gold:Silver has only been above 100 a few times over the last century:

  • 1933 (Great Depression) — 132:1
  • 1940 (WW2) — 100:1
  • 1991 (US Recession) — 100:1
  • 2020 (COVID-19) — 125:1
  • 2025 (???) — 105:1

I'm leaving that last reason blank for now, I'll let historians define that one. Regardless, this move was the fifth time this century that we've seen Gold:Silver breach the 100 level.

Today, that ratio has abated, mostly because of the meteoric rise in silver that came delayed to gold's rise. Silver is a little too volatile for me at these levels, and I think that there are some good strategic moments to take profit on or even short (for the adventurous traders) silver if it continues to rise from here; I covered those in a recent column, "Why Silver Is Running And What Levels To Short At."

Here is a quick and dirty observation of the levels to watch:

Level What It Means
$134 Industrial demand collapses
$125 Industrial copper substitutions are profitable
$91 Peak of Shanghai demand for physical silver
$70 Current psychological floor established by COMEX

If silver continues to run up, as I expect, these are levels at which I would be watching for support and resistance; one should add in round numbers like $100 as a psychological barrier as well.

Here is the current ratio and its decline that came alongside silver's crazy run in the last few months.

This catch-up of silver to gold's price, as opposed to the other way around, is a bullish sign that gold is not overstretched compared to broader monetary demand. I may not have believed that before the CME hiked silver margins a few times to shake out the speculators. There was a drop, but silver has respected that $70 barrier since then.

Conclusion

The drivers of the current bull market in precious metals, institutions, central banks, and traders trying to move risk away from the US and dollar-denominated capital markets are still fueling demand for gold that seems to be indifferent to the price rise. Speculators are only now starting to really pile in, with US ETFs joining in with the major gold buyers, but they are still at tonnages below their levels in 2020.

Gold's breaking away from other commodities, oil in particular, is concerning but does not make me bearish on gold. If anything, the data suggests to me that commodities have some catching up to do and have been suppressed for some time in USD-controlled markets. De-dollarization efforts and lost trust in the US system (particularly after the weaponization of the dollar against the Russians in 2022) have set the stage for multiple more years of gold dominance.

I wouldn't expect forward years to look like the last one, where gold doubled, but I don't expect gold to give up the ground it's taken easily. There is a thick layer of demand behind this push that was absent the last time gold hit a peak, in 2011. The geopolitics and global economic picture were different then, and speculators are not running the show.

I remain long on precious metals via several funds, including GDMN and GLTR as my largest positions.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: The Manitowoc Company: Encouraging Q3 Recovery, But Not A Buy Yet
data.description:
The Thesis

As The Manitowoc Company (MTW) moved into the second half of FY25, it experienced a solid recovery in its EURAF segment, with strong order activity primarily across Europe, resulting in single-digit growth in the consolidated topline in Q3. While recovery is expected to continue in the respective segment with robust demand for tower and mobile cranes in Europe, the core Americas region continues to see headwinds from delayed customer decisions due to uncertainty around tariffs, impacting demand across North America, which could continue to weigh on consolidated topline growth as the company enters FY26.

Meanwhile, margins should remain steady despite modest volume growth as the mix gets more favorable and SG&A costs as a percentage of revenue remain nearly flat. The MTW stock has surged nearly 30% since my last hold rating, primarily due to a rally triggered by a solid Q3 beat about two months back. However, the current price levels appear unsustainable, multiple remain unreasonable, given the persisting underlying weakness in key regions and significantly high leverage, making the MTW stock unattractive for now.

MTW’s Q3 2025 Highlights

MTW has been consistently underperforming since 2024, with contraction across the top and bottom line, which continued further in the first half of FY25 as the demand environment remained mixed with weakness in key regions. However, entering into the second half, the company reported better than expected results for Q3 FY25, finally delivering an increase across the consolidated topline. In the third quarter, MTW’s consolidated topline increased by approximately 5.4% to $553 million.

This growth was primarily driven by a solid 28% jump in the EURAF segment revenue, with significant demand recovery in Europe and robust orders across tower and mobile cranes. While the core Americas segment remains steady with mid-single-digit growth, the MEAP segment remains under pressure as it reported another quarter of double-digit decline in its sales during the last quarter.

While the topline grew nicely, the volume mix was also better during the quarter. This, along with gains from increased sales in the higher-margin aftermarket business, resulted in a 120 bps year-on-year expansion in the adjusted EBITDA margin during the last quarter. Higher EBITDA, along with a slight reduction in other expenses, also benefited the company’s bottom-line performance, as its adjusted EPS came in at $0.14 versus a loss of $0.08 in the same quarter a year ago, notably surpassing the consensus estimates during Q3 FY25.

Outlook

Following a soft start to FY25 with a single-digit decline in the first half, MTW finally delivered growth in its consolidated topline in Q3, as demand conditions improved in certain regions. However, as recovery remains uneven across the regions, topline visibility remains clouded as the company enters FY26.

In the EURAF segment, which accounts for about 30% of total revenue as of Q3, the company experienced a notable improvement during the quarter with a healthy demand environment for the company’s cranes. While mobile crane orders were up 28% year on year, orders for tower crane new machines increased by an impressive 34% in Q3. I expect this order momentum to continue further as customer sentiment continues to improve across Europe, particularly in Germany and France, which are expected to see a strong recovery with improving housing permits in the region. In addition to this, infrastructure-related investments also continue to rise across Europe, which should further support order activity in the quarters ahead. In my view, the EURAF segment should act as a key driver for MTW’s topline improvement as it enters FY26.

However, MTW’s core Americas segment, which still contributes over 55% in total revenue, continues to face headwinds from uncertainty around tariff developments and the impact of recent steel derivative tariffs on specific products in North America. These factors are likely to continue to weigh on customers' purchasing decisions, which could further temper the demand environment for the company’s cranes across the region.

On the other hand, in the MEAP segment, demand trends appear mixed as softness across Asia, particularly China, continued, while the demand in the Middle East regions remains strong. However, it is worth noting that there are signs of recovery across markets, including South Korea, Australia, and Vietnam. Also, there were project wins in Singapore and Hong Kong as well during the quarter, which should gradually support the recovery in the MEAP segment over the quarters ahead. But, given persistent weakness in large markets like China, where new project activity remain largely impacted by ongoing turmoil in its construction markets and broader macro softness, which are expected to continues to weigh on growth in the short term.

Meanwhile, MTW’s outlook beyond FY26 remains relatively constructive as infrastructure investments continue to accelerate across most of the regions, along with early stages of recovery in Europe. MTW also continues to focus on product innovation and its Cranes 50+ strategy, which we have discussed in earlier coverage of MTW, which should fuel topline growth over the long term, with anticipated growth in the aftermarket business and recurring revenue streams in the coming years.

Risks

The key risk to my thesis is faster than expected recovery across MTW's end markets. Although broader softness continued, there was meaningful recovery across Europe with strong support from surge in infrastructure investments, which fueled solid order momentum during Q3, which could accelerate further. On the other hand, the Americas segment might also see demand improvement if tariff-related uncertainty eases over the coming months, unlocking the delayed purchase across the region. In my view, if MTW experienced a quicker rebound across its key regions, it could report a solid Q4, which might lead to a continued rally of MTW's stock further into 2026.

Valuation And Final Verdict

I previously covered the MTW stock in October 2025, maintaining a neutral stance due to its expensive valuation and the company's weak outlook. While the stock continues to trade sideways afterwards, it surged nearly 10% on above-expectation Q3 earnings results in November. And as the rally extended, the stock currently stands at its 52-week high around $13.9%, representing an impressive gain of about 30% since my last hold article, which is notably better than the S&P 500, which grew nearly 4% during the same period.

Currently, the company's stock is trading at a forward Non-GAAP P/E of 42.96, based on its FY25 consensus EPS estimate of $0.33, which looks significantly overvalued when we look at its five-year forward average multiple of 23.39x. However, moving into FY26, we should consider valuation based on FY26 estimates. Based on the FY26 EPS estimates of $0.79, the company's stock trades at a forward P/E of just 17.79, which appears significantly discounted to its historical average.

The FY26 estimate of $0.79 represents a significant jump of over 140% versus FY25 earnings. The company indeed reported a strong beat in Q3 on an adjusted basis, but it is also worth noting that the company has a history of underperforming and this beat came after a long underperformance with seven consecutive quarters of earnings misses.

While the FY26 estimate looks meaningfully strong, I think these are too optimistic, and the chances of further underperformance are high over the coming quarter, given its significantly high leverage at nearly 4x its TTM EBITDA. In addition to this, operating leverage also appears to stay limited due to nearly flat to low single-digit topline growth expectations in the short term, which could further put pressure on the bottom line performance.

In my view, these factors make the valuation unreasonable in the current scenario, and this could further deteriorate in the future as bottom-line prospects remain meaningfully weak despite a slight recovery across the topline and margins. Therefore, despite a cheap forward valuation based on FY26 estimates, I am maintaining a neutral stance and would suggest avoiding this stock until either volume and EBITDA growth get more meaningful or leverage reduces significantly. Hence, I am giving the MTW stock another "HOLD" rating at the current valuation.

data.title: Apple: 2 Takeaways After Switching To The iPhone 17
data.description:
I recently upgraded my smartphone and bought an iPhone 17. The basic version, as I'm not a heavy user. It's an anecdotal reference, but using this new smartphone reinforced two views I had about Apple Inc. (AAPL). The first is about the real loyalty of this system, which is very positive. But the second is one of the reasons for maintaining my sell rating, which is the company's lack of innovation and lack of growth.

First Takeaway: Apple Is Still A Safe Compounder

I haven't been in the Apple ecosystem for very long; my first iPhone was the iPhone 13, which I bought in early 2024/late 2023. After switching to iOS, when I went to replace my laptop, I also opted to buy a MacBook for greater integration. A few months after purchasing the iPhone, I also signed up for a basic iCloud plan. More recently, when I went to replace my smartphone, I didn't even consider an Android device. I just researched which iPhone would be best for my type of use.

Obviously, this is a personal example, but it's something that Apple does very well with its users in general. The average Apple product user ends up “stuck” in this ecosystem for many years, if not forever. Even if the switching costs for some reason (like regulation) decline, I think the ecosystem effect remains rock solid.

Service revenue is another point for Apple to be seen as a safe compounder. Subscriptions, whether to Apple TV, iCloud, or the App Store, are revenues that still have enormous potential, both with Apple managing to create new services and bundle them into very attractive packages and already having distribution in this user base, increasing penetration year after year, as well as creating loyalty and making occasional price increases. To be honest, the “sticky” effect of services such as iCloud is so high that even if the annual adjustment were 5%, it seems extremely unlikely to me that this would cause high churn.

Apple has not been treated as a high-growth company for some time now. The main point of the case is how predictable the demand for its products is, even with these wide moats that allow for a certain price increase over time. In other words, what really matters is the quality of the company, these moats that allow us to envision Apple increasing its cash flow, even if steadily, but for decades and decades, in addition to the various optionalities of this giant user base, if it manages to effectively execute new products.

Second Takeaway: But Apple's Lack Of Innovation Bothers Me

The main argument of my anecdotal evidence is that I only bought the iPhone 17 because I dropped my iPhone 13 and broke the back glass, and since I got a good deal on the 17, I decided to go ahead with this upgrade, which I didn't want to do. In fact, I only didn't buy the iPhone 16 because the store I went to no longer had that model.

What I mean here is that I didn't upgrade because of the improved chip or increased storage, nor did the lack of Apple Intelligence features on the iPhone 13 bother me, especially since there are still very few of them.

Of course, there are positive differences between the two phones, the camera is much better, the dynamic island is useful, but nothing “urgent” that would make me want to switch iPhones more quickly.

And most people who use Apple products are like that. Even though there is a myth about hard-core fans who upgrade as soon as the company releases a new iPhone, users end up upgrading after a few years of use, around five years. Some even argue that three years is not enough.

This goes straight to Apple's lack of innovation. Factors such as physical deterioration of the phone, such as breakage due to misuse, and battery deterioration are organic factors that drive the iPhone cycle. But Apple's internal factors, in theory, also should drive this cycle. This was what the market estimated with Apple Intelligence, as these features would only be available on the iPhone 15 Pro and later models. In theory, everyone using a model inferior to this would want to upgrade. Even though the company has managed to improve its revenue growth recently, it has become clear that these updates have fallen short. Having Apple Intelligence or not is not yet critical for users, and it is still practically a “show me” story.

In fact, as I mentioned in my last article on Apple, I believed that the iPhone Air was one of the positive highlights of innovation because it was trying to do something “different,” but in practice there were problems such as the battery and, in general, negative feedback from the public to the point of having statements such as “virtually no consumer demand,” as mentioned in this Macworld article.

Note that revenues such as those from the iPhone are already very mature, so even though it is very predictable, the company needs to show that there is still room for expansion, that there is room to create new products, create new services, and above all, that it still knows how to execute these strategies well, without promising and not delivering and the like.

Apple’s Rich Valuation: Not The Compound I Want

One of the things I like most about the world of investing is compounding. It is not easy to find a company where investors can be confident enough to hold on to it for a few decades, believing that it will unlock new sources of revenue, increase its prices above inflation, and protect its market through wide moats, but when we find it, it is something that can have the effect of generating generational wealth.

Apple meets almost all of these requirements. As I mentioned in the first part of the article, the Apple ecosystem is truly unmatched. But this long-term compounding, to be interesting, also depends on valuation.

Some may argue that since Apple is a quality company, it doesn't matter what price you pay now as long as you hold on to it long enough, but I completely disagree. If the difference is small, really, in 2040 you won't even remember if you bought Apple for 20x or 22x earnings, and that should have very little impact on your gains. But at larger magnitudes, it matters.

If you bought Apple at 12x earnings or 30x earnings, it will have a substantial impact even if you hold the stock in your portfolio for years. You may be wondering, “12x earnings? That's impossible for Apple.” Well, for now it really is impossible, but 10 years ago it was a reality, and in fact, it was below that multiple that Warren Buffett initiated his position in Apple, since the first purchase was in Q1 2016.

Forget 12x earnings, Apple's profile has really changed, with its revenue mix focused on services and the entire ecosystem that has been built around it. It is difficult to imagine that the company will return to that level. But even so, that does not mean that 35x earnings is the “only choice.” In mid-2023, the company was trading at just over 20x earnings, which would already be a very consistent multiple and open up room for interesting compounding. But currently, the margin of safety is practically non-existent, which not only prevents good compounding but also increases the risk of a downgrade.

You don't have to believe me, you can believe the calculations. In the table below, I assumed that Apple will grow its revenue YoY by 6% every year, without fail. In addition, I considered that every year the net margin increases by 0.5 percentage points, since the revenue mix for services has a higher margin. These are optimistic assumptions, especially considering that in many moments Apple has been unable to grow its revenue. For next year, this gives a P/E of 32x, more or less what the market estimates. But note that in 2036, its earnings yield will still be a measly 6.7%.

The shareholder had to wait 10 years and still only collected a yield slightly above the Treasury, which does not cover the equity risk premium. Even if we consider up to 2030, the earnings yield is still 9%, which is attractive, but not for those who held the stock for almost 15 years.

But now, let's assume there was a correction and the investor bought Apple stock with a valuation of 22x earnings in 2026. Now the yield in 2036 goes to 9.6%, and in 2040 to 12.9%. These are substantial differences in compounding.

For this reason, as I mentioned in this article, to find a fair value for Apple similar to what it is currently trading at in a DCF valuation, it is necessary to use a discount rate of 6% - 7%, which is very low and unattractive in my opinion.

What My Sell Rating Means

I don't think Apple is a bad company; in fact, it is one of the best in the world. But still, I think it is a poor investment case. As I mentioned in the section above, Apple's premium is so high that the market seems to disregard risks and uncertainties such as regulatory issues, which could eventually impact App Store revenue and shake (even if only slightly) the company's ecosystem. This valuation seems to me that the market does not treat Apple only with these reliable and predictable fundamentals, but rather as a bond proxy, and as I have discussed throughout the article, there are some problems, there is the risk of execution, lack of innovation, and so on.

I don't think this is the time to short the company, but rather to avoid it, since there are other better opportunities, and keep it on the radar in case there is an eventual correction that opens up a margin of safety.

Therefore, I maintain my Sell rating for Apple stock.

data.title: CommScope: More Parts Involved In The Sum-Of-The-Parts?
data.description:
Less than three weeks ago, I concluded that investors in CommScope (COMM) were awaiting the closing of the deal with Amphenol (APH) and, related to that, remarks about a special dividend payable as well.

This was actually the second deal with Amphenol, that of a $10.5 billion sale of the Connectivity & Cable Solutions division, as announced last summer. This was a huge and transformative deal, leaving an overleveraged business with a complicated preferred equity position flushed with cash, and frankly gave rise to compelling sum-of-the-parts analysis.

From Overleveraged To Flushed With Cash

Originally founded in 1976, CommScope went public again in 2013 after a private equity adventure in between. A $15 stock around the initial offering peaked around the $40 mark in 2018 as operating performance sputtered, and an M&A-focused strategy left the overleveraged business in trouble. In fact, shares fell to just a dollar in 2024 amidst weak demand post-pandemic, inventory destocking trends by customers, and supply chains opening up again.

Peak sales of around $8 billion had come down to some $5 billion in 2023, with EBITDA of $1 billion being quite solid, translating into solid margins. That was not the issue, but revenue declines and a near $9 billion net debt load created a massive debt overhang.

The company was thrown a financial lifeline in the summer of 2024 when Amphenol acquired the Outdoor Wireless Networks and Distributed Antenna Systems business in a $2.1 billion deal, but amidst continued revenue declines, the business was not out of the woods.

All this changed, as both firms announced a $10.5 billion deal for the connectivity and cable solutions last summer, the second deal in which Amphenol would buy assets of CommScope. With the sum of net debt and preferred equity position standing at $8 billion, that deal would translate into a pro forma net cash position of around $2 billion; that is after factoring in about half a billion in leakage costs. That net cash position would be worth about $9 per share based on 227 million shares outstanding.

Frankly, Amphenol's shareholders could ask if the company should not have tried to acquire CommScope entirely, after all, although Amphenol's track record gives few reasons to complain.

What Is left?

Outside the bag of money, it is interesting to look at RemainCo, which in the case of CommScope consists of Ruckus and ANS.

Ironically, both segments saw strong momentum as well last year. Ruckus posted a 46% increase in second-quarter sales to $190 million, with segment profits reported at $21 million. Revenues of ANS rose as much as 65% to $322 million for that quarter, with segment profits reported at $50 million.

Combined, these units post sales of around $2 billion each year, with operating profits tending to be around $300 million—that is, if we annualize the second quarter performance. Even if these activities were valued at 1 times sales and 7 times operating profits, a valuation of $2 billion would be equal to another $9 per share, for a sum-of-the-part valuation of $18 per share. Based on these hopes, shares rallied to the $14 mark when the deal was announced, gaining some 80% that day alone!

Shares kept gaining ground, having risen to $18 late in December, as the company made progress, including getting shareholder approval for the deal with Amphenol. Third-quarter RemainCo sales were reported at $516 million, that number comparing to $512 million in the second quarter. The issue is that segment profits were posted at $44 million, down from $70 million in the second quarter.

Besides a lower third earnings contribution from RemainCo, the issue is that more pressure was seen. This came after the company guided for full-year RemainCo EBITDA between $350-$375 million, which indicates a fourth-quarter EBITDA number seen between $70 and $75 million, down a bit from a $90 million number in the third quarter, as such setbacks can really impact the valuation of RemainCo.

Given the continued rerating while the RemainCo business was cooling off, I was looking for more clarity, awaiting the deal closing with Amphenol.

Making Inroads

On the 12th of January, the company announced the completion of the divestment of the Connectivity & Cable Solutions business to Amphenol. The company announced that it would pay off all debt and preferred equity and operate with a modest net debt load, sustainable by RemainCo. The special dividend is seen at a minimum of $10 per share, distributed in the coming 60-90 days.

The RemainCo business will be rebranded into the name Vistance Networks, as all this is largely in line with expectations. After all, net proceeds from the sale were pegged at $9 per share, as a $10 per share dividend would result in a very low leverage ratio, all being largely in line with expectations.

The other big news is that the Bloomberg report suggests that Extreme Networks (EXTR) is looking to acquire Ruckus Networks from RemainCo, now called Vistance. A deal could value this business at a billion, with Ruckus being the smaller business here. Based on the third-quarter performance, this business posts sales of around $700 million each year and $80 million in operating earnings, so a billion-dollar deal tag looks quite alright.

Pegging the net cash position pro forma the Amphenol sale at around $9 per share, a $1 billion deal for Ruckus could be worth about $4.50 per share, or more if there is upside to this number. This results in a $13.50 per share valuation, with shares trading at $19.50 per share here. The $6 per share discrepancy comes in at $1.36 billion in dollar terms, which would be attributable to ANS. This business has seen the strongest momentum recently, with the segment posting sales trending around $1.35 billion, with operating profits trending around a hundred million, both based on annualizing the third quarter results.

This implies that these assets are valued at around 1 times sales and about 13 times operating profits. All this looks largely fair here, as a fair amount of overhead and costs are incurred in all these (envisioned) transactions closing.

And Now?

At this point, shares look reasonably valued, although we could see changes given the implications of the Amphenol deal, a potential deal for Ruckus, and the performance of ANS. With shares having gained more ground, now trading near the $20 mark, and the sum-of-the-parts analysis becoming more clear, valuations look quite fair here.

As a result, I am not in a rush to chase the shares here but am keen to keep a close eye on this fascinating story, learning more about ANS, its potential, and handling of overhead as potentially more businesses get sold.
